title: QUIC server-role secp256r1 HelloRetryRequest architecture
purpose: Describe how the managed server-role handshake path detects a retry-eligible first ClientHello for the existing secp256r1-only crypto floor, emits exactly one local HelloRetryRequest, and then rejoins the landed REQ-QUIC-CRT-0112 ServerHello floor only after the retried ClientHello carries the requested secp256r1 key share.
risks: If retry-eligible first ClientHello inputs are not distinguished cleanly from unsupported group-selection failures, the server can silently widen the floor or emit HelloRetryRequest when the peer never advertised secp256r1 support.
If the retry branch publishes ServerHello bytes or Handshake keys before the retried ClientHello arrives, the landed REQ-QUIC-CRT-0112 publication boundary is weakened.
If the retry branch is not bounded to exactly one HelloRetryRequest, repeated or conflicting ClientHello attempts can desynchronize transcript state and overclaim general TLS group negotiation.
design_summary: The transcript-progress owner adds one retry-eligible classification ahead of the landed REQ-QUIC-CRT-0112 ServerHello floor. On the first server-role ClientHello, the parser still enforces the existing TLS 1.3, cipher-suite, and transport-parameter subset, but it distinguishes the case where supported_groups includes secp256r1 while key_share omits a usable secp256r1 entry. On that branch, the bridge driver emits exactly one local HelloRetryRequest requesting secp256r1, records retry-pending state, and keeps ServerHello publication plus Handshake key availability unavailable. The next ClientHello must satisfy the same narrow subset and carry the requested secp256r1 key share; only then does the flow re-enter the landed REQ-QUIC-CRT-0112 ServerHello and Handshake key-publication boundary. Missing secp256r1 support, malformed or conflicting retried ClientHello input, or repeated retry attempts remain deterministic fatal paths.
alternatives_considered: Silently widen the current floor to accept x25519 or hybrid offers. Rejected because the landed REQ-QUIC-CRT-0112 and ARC-QUIC-CRT-0010 subset is explicitly pinned to secp256r1.

Keep returning a fatal alert for the first ClientHello. Rejected because the preserved quic-go evidence shows a retry-capable peer that already advertises secp256r1 in supported_groups, so a bounded HelloRetryRequest path is the smaller interop widening.
Add a native TLS fallback for the retry branch. Rejected because the managed bridge-owned handshake seam remains the design owner for this slice.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The first ClientHello must already be within the landed TLS 1.3 plus TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 subset apart from the missing secp256r1 key_share.
Exactly one HelloRetryRequest may be emitted per handshake attempt.
Handshake key material and local ServerHello publication remain unavailable until the retried ClientHello is accepted.
The slice does not add x25519 acceptance, hybrid named-group acceptance, general TLS group negotiation, 0-RTT, endpoint-host wiring, or interop-runner handshake claims.
